ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dishes — mixed rice and legume preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for dal khichdi; A0CKW (rice dishes) used as closest defensible analogous anchor for a cooked rice-lentil composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for dal khichdi at time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of dal khichdi is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a generous bowl of soft, porridge-like rice and lentil preparation.

nutrient_source.title: Khichdi (Rice and Moong Dal) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods reports khichdi prepared from rice and moong dal. Values adapted for a standard cooked dal khichdi preparation with ghee tadka. Macronutrient profile cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked mung bean and cooked white rice to validate composite estimates. Restaurant portions typically include additional ghee, raising fat content slightly above plain home-cooked values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dal khichdi found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 estimated from the closest analogous entry: cooked basmati/white rice and moong dal composite. Moong dal has a reported GI of approximately 38 (University of Sydney GI Database) and white rice approximately 64–72; a 50:50 cooked weight blend yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 51, classified as low. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 55.5) / 100 ≈ 28.2 for a 300 g serving containing approximately 55.5 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Dal khichdi contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g; a 300 g restaurant serving delivers around 630 mg sodium, representing roughly 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals, so portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice-lentil carbohydrate blend
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 51 and a glycemic load of approximately 28 per standard 300 g serving, dal khichdi produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables or a side salad to blunt the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high soluble fibre from moong dal
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal khichdi provides approximately 1.6 g saturated fat and 1.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g; the soluble fibre from moong dal supports LDL cholesterol reduction, and the dish's low saturated fat content makes it a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal ghee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Khichdi is a traditional Indian one-pot comfort dish made by slow-cooking white rice and split yellow moong dal (mung lentils) together until they reach a soft, porridge-like consistency. The dish is finished with a tadka — a tempering of ghee, cumin seeds, turmeric, ginger, and green chilli — which infuses it with warmth and aroma. It is widely regarded in Indian culinary tradition as a sattvic, easily digestible meal suitable for all ages, convalescents, and those seeking light nourishment. Nutritionally, dal khichdi provides a complementary amino acid profile from the rice-lentil combination, moderate complex carbohydrates, plant-based protein (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), and meaningful amounts of folate, iron, and B vitamins from the moong dal. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 51 places it in the low-GI category, making it a relatively favourable carbohydrate source compared to plain white rice. In Singapore, dal khichdi appears on the menus of North Indian restaurants and is typically served with pickle (achar), papad, or yoghurt. Clinically, it is a moderate-sodium dish that requires portion awareness for hypertensive individuals, while its low saturated fat and soluble fibre content make it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health. For individuals with type 2 diabetes, the moderate glycemic load of a standard serving warrants mindful portioning.
common_user_queries: Is dal khichdi good for diabetes?
How many calories in dal khichdi?
Is khichdi good for weight loss?
What is the GI of khichdi?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for a rice and moong dal khichdi preparation, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data. No exact restaurant-specific lab analysis is available. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for moong dal and white rice components. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved-source entry exactly matches a restaurant-style dal khichdi with ghee tadka.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from input value 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples', as dal khichdi is a rice-lentil staple dish, not a meat or meat-alternative product. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as dal khichdi is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived B12 sources; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA component data. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in ghee-based Indian preparations per USDA data. 7. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for dal khichdi is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the processing date. 8. Multilingual names for Chinese are transliterations as no established Chinese culinary name for dal khichdi exists in common usage; Devanagari Hindi and Tamil script names are standard. 9. Input food_id GD000087 preserved for traceability. 10. Restaurant price data (SGD 16.90) and restaurant names preserved from field survey data source record for traceability but are not used as nutritional evidence.

Removed promotional suffix '(Chef Recommendation)' from food_name to establish canonical English identity.

Added GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values with full estimation documentation.
Added health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance.
